chunks.text: Welcome back to Data Structures Land.

chunks.text: Today, we continue and complete our segment on binary trees.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So this is part two.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: If you missed part one, go back and watch part one.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Last time, we talked about binary trees in general.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: We had each node stored an item, and also a left pointer

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: and a right pointer to other nodes,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: and a parent pointer to another node.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: This was an example of a tree.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: B and C are A's children.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: A is the parents of B and C, and also

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: the root of the entire tree.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: We defined the height of a node.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: We didn't use this too much yet, but we're

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: going to use it a lot today.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So remember, the height is as drawn in red here.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Height of the node is the length of the longest downward path

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: counting edges.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So B, for example, has a length 2 path, so we write a 2 here.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: You can also think of it as if you just

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: live within the subtree rooted at B, B's subtree,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: then what is the maximum depth of those nodes?

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: If you prefer to think about it that way, either way is fine.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And in particular, we distinguished

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: h, the height of the root node, as the height

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: of the entire tree.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And what we achieved last time was basically

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: all of our operations ran in order h time.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So we had subtree insert, subtree delete, subtree

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: first and last.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: We could compute the predecessor and successor of a node,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: all in order h time.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So as long as h was small, we were happy.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And remember, what does predecessor and successor mean?

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: It's talking about an implicit order

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: in the tree, which is what we call traversal order, which

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: is defined recursively as recursively traverse

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: the left subtree, then output the root,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: then recursively traverse the right subtree.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So in this example, the traversal order is f is the,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: if you go all the way left, that was

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: the first in the traversal order.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Then we have some space here.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Then we have d.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Then we have b.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Then we do the right subtree of b, which is e.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Then we have the root, because we finished

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: the left subtree of the root.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So that's a.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And then we have c.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So there's an implicit linear order encoded by this tree.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And the whole point of binary trees

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: is that we can efficiently update the tree much faster

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: than we could explicitly write down

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: an order in an array or something like that.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So binary trees let us quickly.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Now, quickly is not so quick right now,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: because everything is order h.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And in the worst case, h is linear,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: because we could have a tree like this.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: But today, we're going to guarantee that h is log n.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And so the goal of today is to take all of these operations

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: that run in order h time and get them

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: to run in order log n time just by modifying the data

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: structure we've already seen.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So we've done a lot of the hard work.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Just a little bit more work we need

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: to do today in something called AVL trees, or height balance.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: But before we get there, I want to talk a little bit more.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: At the very end of the last lecture,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: we talked about, once you have these subtree operations,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: so I can insert and delete in the subtree,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: how do I actually use that to solve the problems that we

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: care about in this class, which are sequence data structure

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: and set data structure?

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So we talked mostly about the set data structure last time.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So in general, we're going to define what traversal order

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: we maintain by a binary tree.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And so for a set, because for the set interface,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: we're interested in doing queries like find next

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: and find previous, given a key.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: If it's not there, tell me the previous one or the next one.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: This is something we could do with binary search.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And so the big cool thing that binary trees let us do,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: if we let the traversal order always

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: be all of the items stored in increasing key order,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: then we are effectively maintaining the items in order

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: in the traversal order sense.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Again, we're not explicitly maintaining them in order.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: But up here, we're maintaining a tree that

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: represents items in key order.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And so this lets us do a subtree find operation,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: which you could easily use to implement find and find

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: previous and so on.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: As follows, we start at the root of the tree.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So we can say node equals root initially.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And then we can recursively search for a key k as follows.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: We check, well, if the item at the root

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: has a key that's bigger than k.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Let me draw a little picture.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So we're at some node here.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: This is a node.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And it has a left subtree and a right subtree.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And there's some item with some key.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So if the key we're looking for is less than the node's item,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: that means it's down here in the left subtree.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And so we recurse on node.left.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: If they're equal, that means that this item

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: is the item we're looking for.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So we can just return it or the node,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: depending on what you're looking for.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And if the key in here is greater

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: than the key we're looking for, then we'll recurse to the right.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: If you think about it a little bit,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: this is exactly binary search on an array.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: It just happens to be on a tree instead.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: If you think of an array like this,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: where does binary search do?

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: It first looks at the key in the middle.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: I'm going to draw that as the root.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And then it recurses either on the left chunk,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: which I will draw recursively, or on the right chunk.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And so if you happen to have a perfect binary tree

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: like this one, it is simulating exactly binary search

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: in this array.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: But this we're going to be able to maintain dynamically, not

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: perfect anymore, but close, whereas this we could not

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: maintain in sorted order.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So this is like a generalization of binary search

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: to work on trees instead of on arrays.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And for this reason, set binary trees

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: are called binary search trees because they are the tree

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: version of binary search.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So there's many equivalent names.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So binary search tree is another name for set binary tree.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And the key thing that makes this algorithm work

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: is the so-called binary search tree property,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: which is all the keys in the left subtree of a node

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: are less than the root of that node.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And that key is less than all the keys in the right subtree.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And this is true recursively all the way down.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And so that's how you prove that this algorithm is

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: correct by this property.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Why is this true?

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Because if we can maintain traversal order

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: to be increasing key, then that's

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: exactly what traversal order means.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: It tells you all the things in the left subtree

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: come before the root, which come before all the things

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: in the right subtree.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So this property implies this one.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And how do you maintain things in increasing key order?

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: It's pretty easy.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: If you want to insert an item, where does it belong?

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Well, you do this search to find where it

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: would belong if it was there.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: If it's there, you can overwrite the value stored with that key.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: If it's not, this search will fall off the tree at some point.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And that's where you insert a new node in your tree.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: That was covered in recitation, so I don't want to dwell on it.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: What I want to focus on today is the other application.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: This is for representing a set, which is relatively easy.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: A challenge we've sort of set ourselves up for,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: but we need a little more work, is

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: to make sequence binary trees.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So suppose I have a binary tree.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: And what I would like, I mentioned

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: at the end of last time, is that I

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: want the traversal order of my tree to be the sequence order,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: the order that I'm trying to represent that's changed

chunks.title: Lec

chunks.title: Lec

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: But now I have to think about, how do I do a search?

chunks.title: Lec

chunks.text: And here is an algorithm for what I would like to work,

chunks.title: Lec

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So suppose I give you a subtree specified by a node.

chunks.title: Lec

chunks.title: Lec

chunks.text: If I ask for i equals the size of the tree minus 1,

chunks.text: I want to get the rightmost descendant.

chunks.text: That was the first and last in the subtree

chunks.text: that we talked about.

chunks.text: But we know how to find the first and last,

chunks.text: Let's just assume that I knew that magically

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: in constant time.

chunks.text: OK.

chunks.text: Then I claim that the size of the left subtree,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: so why don't I expand this diagram a little bit.

chunks.text: So we have node as before.

chunks.text: Call subtree at of node.left, i.

chunks.text: If i equals nl, think about it for a second.

chunks.text: So nl is the number of nodes here.

chunks.text: And so that means this node has index nl.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: The index of this node is nl.

chunks.text: And so if the one index we're looking for is that one,

chunks.text: And that means that the node we're looking for

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So if we define it to be sequence order,

chunks.text: then we know all the things that come

chunks.title: Lec

chunks.text: Now, when we recurse down here, our numbering system

chunks.text: So we need to do a little bit of subtraction

chunks.text: Minus nl for these guys, minus 1 for the root node.

chunks.text: And that will give us the index we're looking

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: for within this subtree.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So my point is this algorithm is basically

chunks.title: Lec

chunks.text: And this algorithm gives it to you exactly the same way

chunks.text: So the idea with subtree augmentation
